feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The email discussion raises significant concerns about the security vulnerabilities associated with STARK verification in Bitcoin nodes. Specifically, it points out that if the verification time is as long as 1.8 seconds of CPU-time, as initially stated, it presents a considerable risk for denial-of-service (DOS) attacks. This vulnerability stems from the potential for attackers to send complex STARKs that require extensive CPU processing time, thereby overwhelming the system.
Moreover, the debate extends to the challenges in standardizing zero-knowledge virtual machines (zkVMs) or circuits. The inherent complexity in these systems makes them difficult to standardize without relying on arbitrary code versioning. This complication is exacerbated by the fact that any bug within a circuit cannot be rectified simply; instead, it would necessitate a complete overhaul of the verification process. This re-emphasizes the risks and technical hurdles in implementing such advanced cryptographic solutions in environments where security and efficiency are paramount.
Additionally, the argument touches upon the theoretical possibility of compressing the entire Bitcoin chain into a few kilobytes of data using a recursive STARK. However, it also highlights that the complexity and DOS attack surface remains significantly high, comparable to dealing with a single proof. This suggests that while recursive proofs could theoretically reduce data size, they do not diminish the risks associated with DOS attacks, thus undermining their practical utility in this context.
